blocks.text: A2UI is **transport-agnostic by design**. It defines the JSON message structure and the semantic contract between server and client — not how those bytes travel. A transport just needs three things: reliable, in-order delivery; clear message framing; and a way to carry metadata (for capability exchange and, if used, client data-model sync).

blocks.caption: Named transport bindings in the v1.0 spec
rows: The standard binding for agent-to-user interaction; convenient framework integrations
A dedicated extension mapping A2UI envelopes into A2A protocol messages
A2UI carried over MCP tool calls, tool outputs, or resource subscriptions
General-purpose streaming or request/response, no special integration

blocks.title: The one-line version
blocks.text: MCP is one of several possible carriers for the A2UI message stream. A2UI is not built into MCP, and MCP is not required to use A2UI.
A2UI and MCP Apps — complementary, not competing

This is genuinely easy to conflate, so it's worth stating precisely, sourced from Google's own joint post with the MCP Apps team: **MCP Apps** renders creative-freedom iframes using standard web tech — flexible, but with real hurdles in performance and security isolation. **A2UI** renders natively through the host's own component library — consistent and secure, but the agent is restricted to whatever's in the active catalog. Neither is a superset of the other.

blocks.caption: Three named integration patterns
rows: Serve A2UI payloads as MCP Resources or via MCP Tool calls — native rendering, no iframe at all
Embed an MCP App (iframe) inside an A2UI component for a state-heavy custom widget
An MCP App ships its own A2UI renderer, injecting dynamic agent-driven UI into an existing iframe app

blocks.title: What this site's own demos actually are
blocks.text: Worth being precise about, since you're reading this inside exactly this setup: a2uicatalog's MCP Apps surface runs its own **blocks-dialect renderer inside an MCP Apps sandboxed iframe** (the ui/initialize handshake, tool-result delivery). That is NOT literally a stream of A2UI protocol messages arriving over MCP — it's a2uicatalog's own dialect, rendered natively in-page. The catalogue's a2ui-emit tool can convert that same content into real, conformant A2UI v1.0 createSurface messages on request — the two are related but genuinely distinct paths, and this brief has been careful to name which one it's teaching at each point.

blocks.question: Is A2UI a layer that sits inside MCP, requiring MCP to function?
options: Yes — A2UI messages are always MCP tool outputs
No — A2UI is transport-agnostic; MCP is one of several optional carriers (AG-UI, A2A, SSE, WebSockets, REST are others)
Yes, but only for MCP Apps specifically
No — A2UI predates and has no relationship to MCP at all
blocks.explanation: A2UI defines the message/component contract independent of transport. MCP carrying A2UI ("A2UI over MCP") is one recognized pattern among several named transport bindings — not a dependency.

points: A2UI defines the message contract, not the transport — AG-UI, A2A, MCP, SSE, WebSockets, and REST are all valid carriers
A2UI and MCP Apps solve different problems (safe native rendering vs creative-freedom iframes) and are designed to compose, not compete
Three named integration patterns exist: A2UI over MCP, MCP Apps in A2UI, A2UI in MCP Apps
a2uicatalog's own MCP Apps demos use its blocks dialect inside the MCP Apps handshake — not raw A2UI streaming, though convertible via a2ui-emit
